item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fuck lmao +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree with your second statement, not all humorous articles have to be -Js.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This should be a -J.

Also, it doesn't really feel anomolous until the drone incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I do like most of the stuff Hippo writes, this SCP-fication of current gaming industry malpractices is not really my cup of tea.

item.title: Re: While humorous...
item.content:encoded:
It being a Joke article would take away a lot of weight for the article.

A key part SCP-J articles is that they're not "real." Yes there Is No Canon, but Joke articles are even less canon than most, it's implicit that the Foundation is in no way actually containing a rock that makes you procrastinate, a demon that keeps telling you how to contain it, or Sephiroth, the articles aren't "real" even in their own story.

This article isn't just a Video Game joke, but it's also commentary on the military industrial complex and the privatization of war. The article isn't a "joke" in universe because it's a serious dangerous problem that the Foundation needs to contain. Making the article not "real" by appending the J to the end would remove this bit of real world commentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has a similar vibe to SCP-4533, I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, fuck. This is way better than my take on anomalous microtransactions. +1.

item.title: While humorous...
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't this fit better as a -J?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And THIS is why we need net neutrality.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is mentioned earlier, and intended as a hint of where this is going (allowing corporations to sponsor drone strikes).

Makes sense. I think I've read this part in a bit rushed way, so I didn't catch this immediately.

This part still feels kinda random to me, perhaps because it has a different theme from the rest of the article. I mean that McDonald's part seems to convey a more broad and general topic of corporations, while the central focus of the article itself is military conflicts and… Well, capitalizing on them. I see the connection there, but the last addendum doesn't go far enough in portraying the topic that's mentioned in it, so it feels out of place.

Well, at least that's my impression of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, capitalism is bad — but more to your point:

According to reports from the Pentagon, Mediagen had expressed interest in the monetization of conflict and the privatization of the military through the use of micro-transactions and corporate sponsorships.

This is mentioned earlier, and intended as a hint of where this is going (allowing corporations to sponsor drone strikes).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed it for the most part, but the last log seemed a bit confusing. Is there another meaning to McDonald's involvement with this other than "capitalism bad" and I missed it?

Still, I think I'll upvote in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hippo…please, write a book. I will buy it!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 would feel a sense of pride and accomplishment again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: appreciative you kept the mcdonald's drone strike you mentioned in chat +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this could honestly be the plot to an episode of black mirror or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is probably the issue. I think that if you wanted to talk about corporate drone strikes, it would have been better to show why they are disturbing and bad - it might seem obvious that McDonalds drone striking people would be bad, but the article doesn't really make that link, either in that this would provide an additional incentive for hyper militarism or that having your house blown up by a mcflurry missile would be an especially depressing, alienating late capitalism experience. The fairly bland ending of some nameless chaos insurgency guys getting droned and a soundbite playing only lends itself to the 'lol video game ads' reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's worth anything, I also read this as more being about game lootboxes than the insane thinking that quests toward maximum efficiency produce. Additional focus on the latter would have felt more compelling to me, and I think would have a stronger through-line than what currently feels extremely focused on one element of the broad phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fair — keep in mind, I'm out of the loop of this stuff (I've never even played a game with lootboxes!), and for me, the really interesting question was less in the video-game parallels (which struck me as a way to make this fun) and more in the real-life parallels (the idea of private security firms having their employees purchase their own weapons and bullets… from the firm itself. "Company store" — but applied to the military).

Like, a world where we've coupled our military complex so deeply to corporate interests that corporations can sponsor drone-strikes (or where soldiers are charged by the bullet to maximize efficiency) is a thing that legit gives me anxiety. I might have gone a little too deep into the 'lol micro-transactions' end of this for that anxiety to really emerge, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting to enjoy this more based on the themes and the expectation of quality from Hippo, but I think I'm burned out on this idea after an endless stream of reddit posts and memes about how microtransactions are bad. It also evokes unpleasant memories of 2000s video game webcomics based on 'what if video game thing, but real?' without going anywhere with it.

item.title: God damnit Hippo
item.content:encoded:
VOICE: — provide soldiers with a sense of pride and accomplishment for unlocking —

Absolute madman.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback — I was a little leery about it at first, but I think the additional log (as per your suggestion) works as a nice segue from the first log to the final drone incident.

Plus, it let me work in the classic "provide players with a sense of pride and accomplishment for unlocking different heroes" line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that this has a great satirical hook but it's not pushing that hook as far as it should be to get a belly laugh out of me. One or two more incidents or logs in-between the McDonalds drone strike and the upgrade package would work really well.

EDIT: and there we go. I think it works now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That log though..
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've actually been thinking about the idea of real-life pay-walls for military gear for a while, now (ever since I read about Activision's loot-boxes). I brought it up again in chat yesterday, and a few people threw out some ideas that helped me finally sit down and hammer it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this idea was proposed in chat literally yesterday. This was fast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"medical devices with built-in pay-walls" oh good lord.

this is a great mix of funny and alarming; it dances so closely with how the world actually works that even the most absurd bits are genuinely unnerving.

+1

item.title: we left syria b/c we couldn't afford the middle-east DLC
item.content:encoded:
ba da ba ba baaaa --
i'm lovin' it ~

SOURCES:

  • Image here.
  • Based on the very real Land Warrior and Future Force Warrior projects (yes, these were real).
  • Also based on Activision's lootbox and pay-to-win schemes (Mediagen is just a cropped version of their old name, Mediagenic).
  • A lot of people gave me a lot of great feedback for this (too many to remember), but I'll at least point out that CadaverCommander (SCP-3982) and JackalRelated (SCP-4917) provided me with a mishmash of fantastic ideas (only a fraction of which actually made it into the article).

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
